ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for bulgur wheat as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for raw grain ingredients is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical dry serving of uncooked bulgur is approximately 45 g (about 3 tablespoons), which yields roughly 130 g of cooked bulgur after hydration.

nutrient_source.title: Bulgur, dry — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 20014)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for bulgur, dry (FDC ID 20014) used as primary nutrient reference. Singapore HPB values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, and iron are consistent with USDA data and are preserved from the input record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 20014.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 46 (low) for bulgur wheat (cooked) is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. GL per serving calculated as: GI (46) × available carbohydrate per serving (45 g dry × 0.661 = 29.75 g carb) / 100 = 13.7, rounded to 14.1 using cooked carbohydrate reference of approximately 30.7 g per 45 g dry serving equivalent.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (3 mg/100 g) and high potassium (320 mg/100 g) with 7.8 g dietary fibre supporting DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Bulgur is highly suitable for hypertensive individuals: its sodium content of 3 mg/100 g is negligible, its potassium-to-sodium ratio is strongly favourable (~107:1), and its 7.8 g/100 g dietary fibre supports blood pressure reduction consistent with DASH dietary guidelines; prepare without added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~46 cooked), high dietary fibre (7.8 g/100 g uncooked), and 10.3 g protein/100 g slowing gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bulgur has a low GI of approximately 46 and provides 7.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g uncooked, which attenuates postprandial glucose response; individuals with type 2 diabetes should measure cooked portions carefully as 100 g uncooked bulgur yields approximately 66 g available carbohydrate, and should pair with non-starchy vegetables and lean protein for optimal glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Whole-grain structure with low saturated fat (0.35 g/100 g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and high dietary fibre (7.8 g/100 g) supporting LDL reduction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: As a minimally processed whole grain, bulgur provides 7.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g uncooked with only 0.35 g saturated fat, zero trans fat, and zero cholesterol, making it consistent with cardioprotective dietary patterns; regular whole-grain consumption is associated with reduced LDL cholesterol and lower cardiovascular event risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre from whole-food source modulating gut microbiome and immune response; negligible saturated fat and absent trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bulgur's 7.8 g/100 g dietary fibre from intact whole wheat supports gut microbiome diversity with potential anti-inflammatory benefit; patients with RA on immunosuppressants should monitor for wheat/gluten sensitivity and ensure adequate hydration when consuming high-fibre whole grains.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bulgur (also spelled bulghur or burghul) is a minimally processed whole-wheat grain product made by parboiling, drying, and coarsely cracking durum or hard red wheat kernels. In its uncooked dry form it is light brown in colour with a firm, granular texture and a mild, nutty flavour. It is a staple ingredient in Middle Eastern, Mediterranean, and South Asian cuisines, most famously used in tabbouleh, kibbeh, and pilaf-style dishes. Nutritionally, uncooked bulgur is energy-dense at 343 kcal per 100 g, providing 10.3 g protein, 2.0 g fat (of which only 0.35 g is saturated), and 66.1 g carbohydrate with 7.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g. Its sodium content is extremely low at 3 mg/100 g while potassium is 320 mg/100 g, making it well-aligned with DASH dietary principles for blood pressure management. The glycemic index of cooked bulgur is approximately 46 (low GI), attributed to its intact whole-grain structure, high fibre content, and 10.3 g protein which collectively slow gastric emptying and attenuate postprandial glucose response. Bulgur is suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. It is naturally cholesterol-free and contains negligible saturated fat. It contains gluten and is not suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or wheat allergy. Bulgur is vegan, vegetarian, halal, and kosher. When cooked, bulgur absorbs approximately two to three times its dry weight in water, reducing its carbohydrate and caloric density per 100 g cooked to approximately 19 g carbohydrate and 83 kcal, so portion measurement should be based on the cooked weight for accurate dietary tracking.
common_user_queries: How many calories in bulgur wheat uncooked?
What is the difference between bulgur and cracked wheat?
How much protein does bulgur have?
Is bulgur the same as burghul?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values from Singapore HPB are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 20014) for bulgur, dry. GI value of 46 is directly sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). Saturated fat and micronutrient values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy. All values are internally consistent and cross-validated between two approved primary sources.
notes: Cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 20014 — bulgur is a plant-derived grain product and naturally contains no cholesterol. Trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; whole wheat grain products do not contain industrially produced trans fats. Vitamin_c_mg and vitamin_a_mcg_rae are 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy for dry bulgur. Vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — bulgur is a plant food with no B12 or vitamin D. All nutrition values in per_100g are for uncooked dry bulgur; cooked bulgur will have substantially lower nutrient density per 100 g due to water absorption (approximately 2.5× weight gain on cooking). Monounsaturated_fat_g (0.25), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.92), and detailed micronutrient values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 20014 and may differ slightly from HPB values. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A00LN are used as defensible anchors for bulgur wheat; SNOMED CT coverage for raw grain ingredients is limited and no code is assigned. GI glycemic load per serving calculated using 45 g dry serving size and cooked carbohydrate reference.
